items.q: If an object is moving at constant velocity in a straight line, what is the net force on it?
items.correct: Zero — by Newton's first law, constant velocity requires zero net force.
items.misconception: There must be a forward force on the object to keep it moving.
items.random_wrong: The net force equals the velocity times the mass of the object.
items.why: This is the classic 'impetus' misconception — everyday experience (objects slow down due to friction) leads students to believe motion requires a continuous force.

items.q: In a collision between two objects, what quantity is always conserved (in a closed system)?
items.correct: Total momentum is always conserved; kinetic energy is only conserved in elastic collisions.
items.misconception: Kinetic energy is always conserved in collisions because energy is conserved.
items.random_wrong: Velocity is always conserved in any collision.
items.why: Students conflate momentum conservation with energy conservation; they overlook that energy can become heat/sound in inelastic collisions.

items.q: After one half-life, what happens to an individual radioactive nucleus?
items.correct: It has a 50 percent chance of having decayed — radioactive decay is random for any single nucleus.
items.misconception: Every nucleus is guaranteed to have half-decayed after one half-life.
items.random_wrong: The nucleus shrinks to half its original size after one half-life.
items.why: Students treat half-life as deterministic for individual atoms, when it is a statistical statement about large populations of nuclei.

items.q: Redshift of distant galaxies is evidence that:
items.why: Correct answer: the universe is expanding. “the universe is contracting” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The Sun is currently in which stage?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Main sequence. “Protostar” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If the universe is 13.8 billion years old, how many seconds is that? (1 year ≈ 3.15×10⁷ s; answer in 10¹⁷ s)
items.why: Exact answer: 4.35 x10^17 s. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
